data.title: TBIL: The Fed Has Cut, What Is Next (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

The US Treasury 3-Month Bill ETF (NASDAQ:TBIL) is a $6 billion fund and a reference point in the short term treasury fund cohort. The instrument is one of the most utilized cash parking vehicles, and we have covered this ETF before with a 'Buy' rating.

With the much anticipated Fed meeting from September 17, 2025, now behind us and a 25 bps cut, we are going to revisit TBIL and outline for investors what the recent cut means.

Buying T-Bills via an ETF

As a reminder, TBIL strives to provide exposure to the current 3-month Treasury Bill and thus represents a play on the front end of the yield curve. Its composition is reflective of that:

Please note the ETF holds three issuances, with the maximum maturity being December 2025.

Retail investors have the ability to buy T-Bills outright, but they have to go through the operational process of rolling them upon expiration. TBIL does that for you automatically, although there is a slight tax tweak one has to be aware of. This writer learned it the hard way when doing taxes - while outright T-Bills are reported automatically by brokerage companies as having exempt interest from state and local taxes, ETFs like TBIL need to have a manual calculation that needs inputting into tax software. Outside this slight reporting tweak, the fund represents a similar exposure to just rolling 3-month T-Bills.

If we take a look at historic correlations, we can see the one-for-one impact when Fed Funds are lowered:

The blue line in the chart above represents the Federal Funds Effective Rate, while the green line is the 3-month constant maturity T-Bills. Note the almost perfect correlation, with very short periods of very slight yield discrepancies. In essence this graph tells us that as the Fed cuts, 3-month T-Bills will follow Fed Funds lower.

The Fed has cut; what is next?

The Fed disappointed some market participants by having only a 25 bps cut, but two more cuts are priced in for the rest of 2025:

WASHINGTON – The Federal Reserve on Wednesday approved a widely anticipated rate cut and signaled that two more are on the way before the end of the year as concerns intensified over the U.S. labor market even as inflation is still in the air.

In an 11-to-1 vote signaling less dissent than Wall Street had anticipated, the Federal Open Market Committee lowered its benchmark overnight lending rate by a quarter percentage point. The decision puts the overnight funds rate in a range between 4.00%-4.25%.

Chairman Powell mentioned that the decisions in the past regarding 50 bps cuts or increases were driven by the narrative of the Fed being behind what they thought would be an appropriate monetary stance. The current macro framework was described as being balanced, with less labor available balancing out less job availabilities:

“The marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers is unusual in this less dynamic and somewhat softer labor market,” he said. “The downside risks to employment appear to have risen.”

The topic of inflation was also discussed, with the Fed base-case including a one-time price increase from tariffs, rather than continuous inflation causing price moves.

While we can discuss and debate where monetary policy should be, one thing is for certain, and that is what the market is now pricing in:

The above graph plots the futures market for 1-month SOFR and the Fed Dot Plot. While the 'Dot Plot' is subject to debate, change, and political nominations, the SOFR futures market is not. The market is showing us deep cuts to come in the next year, with 1-month SOFR at 3% come December 2026.

We stated that the Fed Dot Plot is subject to various political nominations because we saw an interesting development in the latest Fed release. While most of the projections were quite similar, there was a Fed member who plotted an astounding 125 bps of cuts for the rest of 2025:

Only one official called for no cuts, while another member of the FOMC thought the central bank should cut rates by an additional 1.25% percentage point by the end of 2025. Because the dot plot is anonymous we don’t know for sure which member of the FOMC made each of these predictions. But given calls by the Administration for a significant cut in interest rates, it seems likely it was made by one of the President’s recent appointments.

The takeaway here is that the 'Dot Plot' projections are fickle, and investors are better served by looking at what the futures market is telling us.

Impact to TBIL

TBIL will see its 30-day SEC yield decrease as per the latest cut and will continue to embed almost one-for-one any future rate decreases. If indeed Fed Funds are at 3% by the end of 2026, do expect TBIL to yield something very similar at that point in time.

TBIL is purely an automated way to get exposure to short-dated T-Bills and the front of the yield curve and thus represents an almost direct take on Fed Funds. Should we be happy that TBIL will yield less? No. Is it in-line with what the fund is supposed to do? Absolutely.

Investors looking for protection from decreasing Fed Funds should look for longer-duration names, and we have covered such funds in the past:

  • SHY: Smart Duration Play, 4.9% Yield
  • NEAR: Still A Buy After The Rise In Rates

While there are other short-term funds that offer higher yields, they do come with either asset default risk or equity risk. Investors looking for clean treasury exposure should look only at higher-duration names.

Conclusion

TBIL is a cash parking vehicle composed of T-Bills. With a negligible duration and an almost perfect correlation to Fed Funds, the ETF represents a take on the front end of the yield curve. With the most recent Fed cut, we are going to see a lowering in the dividend here. Expect this dynamic to continue in the future as well - if we do get to 3% Fed Funds by the end of 2026, as the SOFR futures market is telling us, do expect the TBIL 30-day dividend yield to move down to that level as well. Investors looking for higher yields and protection from cuts should look at SHY and NEAR.

data.title: Manulife: Buybacks And 10% Earnings Yield Make It A Buy
data.description:
Interest rate cuts can be great for borrowers but not so much for savers, as lower rates make money market funds and short-dated bonds less attractive. Such may be the case now, as the Federal Reserve cut rates by a quarter point and signaled potential for two more cuts coming this year. As such, dividend stocks that offer growing payouts and a decent yield could catch a bid from the $7 trillion of liquid assets sitting on the sidelines in money market funds.

This brings me to Manulife Financial (NYSE:MFC), which I last covered in June, highlighting its robust earnings growth and catalysts from high-margin wealth management business. While MFC’s current price of $31 isn’t far off from its 52-week high of $33, it remains attractively valued with a 10x forward PE and a 4.1% dividend yield.

In this article, I revisit MFC including recent business fundamentals, and discuss why it’s currently a ‘Buy’ for its value, income, and growth potential, so let’s get started!

Why MFC?

Manulife Financial is a large insurance and wealth management company based in Canada, with a footprint spanning Asia, Canada, and the United States. It has a 130-year history, and has evolved into a digitally enabled, globally diversified insurer and asset manager. MFC’s product portfolio includes life insurance, wealth and asset management, retirement solutions, and reinsurance businesses.

MFC’s diversification, particularly in its exposure to Asia wealth management, has resulted in robust shareholder returns. This is reflected by MFC’s 209% total return over the past 10 years. While this trails the 243% of the S&P 500 (SPY), it handily beats the 172% and 165% of peers Canadian insurance peers Sun Life Financial (SLF) and Great-West LifeCo (OTCPK:GWLIF), as shown below.

MFC continued to deliver strong momentum in Q2 2025, with APE (annualized premium equivalent) sales growth of 15% YoY. This was driven by strong contributions from Asia and the U.S. at 31% and 40%, respectively. Encouragingly, all of MFC’s insurance segments delivered over 30% growth in new business CSM (contractual service margin), which is a key indicator of future earnings power. Plus, new business value rose by a robust 20% YoY. As shown below, Asia has demonstrated strong momentum across all fronts over the past 12 months.

At the same time MFC’s Global WAM (wealth and asset management) arm delivered its seventh consecutive quarter of +20% YoY growth in pretax core earnings. Notably, net inflows reached nearly $1 billion, driven by institutional and retirement channels and core EBITDA margin expanded to 30.1%.

These strong results led to book value per share growth of 7% YoY to C$35.78, even after returning C$6.4 billion to shareholders through stock buybacks and dividends. As shown below, MFC has repurchased a significant 10% of its outstanding shares over the past 3 years.

Looking ahead, MFC is well-positioned for the fast-growing alternative asset management segment through its agreement to acquire a 75% stake in Comvest Credit Partners. It plans to have full ownership of this business in six years. Comvest is a private credit manager with $14.7 billion in AUM and will expand MFC’s already robust alternatives platform to nearly $100 billion. Synergies from this deal include MFC’s ability to leverage its 19 million clients and global distribution channels, particularly in Asia, to scale Comvest’s offerings.

Additionally, Asia remains a strong growth driver for MFC due to expanding middle-class demand for insurance and wealth products. MFC also maintains a conservative balance sheet and 96% of fixed income holdings are investment grade. This well insulates it against credit shocks.

MFC carries a strong ‘A’ credit rating from S&P and this is supported by a 136% LICAT ratio, sitting well ahead of the 100% minimum requirement set by Canada’s financial regulator. This supports MFC’s 4.1% dividend yield, which is well-protected by a 43% payout ratio, leaving plenty of retained capital for business expansion and share buybacks. The dividend also comes with an 8.6% 5-year CAGR and 11 consecutive years of growth.

Lastly, I find value in MFC at the current price of $31.03 with a forward PE of 10.7, sitting far below its historical PE of 15.5, as shown below.

At the current valuation, the market does not seem to fully appreciate MFC’s growth potential in Global WAM and upside potential in Asia. Plus, it’s getting near 10% earnings yield on share buybacks at the current price. Over the medium term, I expect for MFC to be able to achieve 7-9% EPS growth annually, including accretion from share buybacks. This, combined with the 4.1% yield and potential for a reversion to the mean valuation could result in double-digit total returns from here.

Risks to MFC include potential for a tougher business climate in Canada and the U.S. due to a slow job market. This is reflected by an $82 million after-tax provision for expected credit losses during Q2. Moreover, MFC’s annuities business faces longevity risks, should policyholders live significantly longer than what actuarial models predict. Plus, volatility in equity markets could reduce MFC’s fee revenue based on AUM.

Investor Takeaway

Manulife Financial presents a compelling opportunity for value and income investors. Its 4.1% dividend yield, strong capital position, and consistent buybacks offer an attractive proposition. MFC has robust momentum in its core businesses, particularly in Asia, and in and global wealth management. Its strategic move into private credit via Comvest Credit Partners expands its alternatives platform and positions it for long-term growth.

Lastly, MFC trades at a discounted 10.7x forward PE that’s well below its historical average. As such, MFC remains undervalued for its consistent performance and upside potential, making it a solid 'Buy' for income, value, and growth.

data.title: AAII Sentiment Survey: Optimism Skyrockets
data.description:
Optimism among individual investors about the short-term outlook for stocks increased in the latest AAII Sentiment Survey. Meanwhile, neutral sentiment and pessimism decreased.

Bullish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will rise over the next six months, increased 13.6 percentage points to 41.7%. Bullish sentiment is above its historical average of 37.5% for the first time in seven weeks.

Neutral sentiment, expectations that stock prices will stay essentially unchanged over the next six months, decreased 6.5 percentage points to 16.0%. Neutral sentiment is unusually low and is below its historical average of 31.5% for the 61st time in 63 weeks. This is the lowest neutral sentiment reading in five months, since April 10, 2025, when it was 12.5%. Today's neutral sentiment reading ranks among the 50th lowest in the survey's history.

Bearish sentiment, expectations that stock prices will fall over the next six months, decreased 7.1 percentage points to 42.4%. Bearish sentiment is unusually high and is above its historical average of 31.0% for the 42nd time in 44 weeks.

The bull-bear spread (bullish minus bearish sentiment) increased 20.8 percentage points to –0.7%. The bull-bear spread is below its historical average of 6.5% for the 31st time in 33 weeks.

This week’s special question asked AAII members where they would put the odds of a recession occurring before the end of this year.

Here is how they responded:

  • Highly likely: 4.2%.
  • A higher-than-typical chance: 26.9%.
  • Not more or less likely than I would normally expect: 26.9%.
  • Unlikely: 39.6%.
  • Not sure: 2.3%.

This week’s Sentiment Survey results:

  • Bullish: 41.7%, up 13.6 percentage points.
  • Neutral: 16.0%, down 6.5 percentage points.
  • Bearish: 42.4%, down 7.1 percentage points.

Historical averages:

  • Bullish: 37.5%.
  • Neutral: 31.5%.
  • Bearish: 31.0%.

The AAII Sentiment Survey has been conducted weekly since July 1987. The survey and its results are available online.

If you want to become an effective manager of your own assets and achieve your financial goals, consider a risk-free 30-day Trial AAII Membership.

data.title: Prysmian: 2X In 5 Months, What's Next With A ~$20B Backlog?
data.description:
Prysmian: Wiring Major Megatrends

All financial figures are in € unless otherwise stated.

Two investments have delivered tremendous alpha to my portfolio this year: Alphabet (GOOG; GOOGL) and Prysmian (OTCPK:PRYMF; OTCPK:PRYMY). I wrote about the former two months ago with a "Strong Buy" rating, basically suggesting that we were buying Google Search for free. We all know that the stock took off, also thanks to a favorable ruling on the Chrome divestiture case. The latter, however, is not known by many, even though it is the world's largest cable manufacturer and provider of cabling solutions. As I argued a year ago, the company is the perfect pick-and-shovel play as the AI revolution unfolds.

Macro Backdrop

Energy And Compute

If you read Seeking Alpha, you have probably already learned quite a bit about these two revolutions. I, too, addressed them thoroughly in my recent article "Capex Nation." In short, the International Energy Agency (IEA) has often said that annual investments in electricity grids need to double to at least $800 billion by 2030 to align with net-zero goals. The renewable queue is over 1,500 GW because renewables are stuck in interconnection queues that - guess what? - need cabling. This is music for Prysmian's shareholders. In Europe, the REPowerEU program is a matter of security and deploys around EUR 210 billion by 2027 for renewable energy and grids. The U.S. Department of Energy is also committed to $7.6 billion of federal investment for 105 grid projects. But the U.S. Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act is also pouring money in this direction to upgrade grids and offshore wind. But, even more importantly, data centers are booming, and the IEA projects that by next year, we will see energy demand above 1,000TWh.

Rates

The Fed finally cut interest rates, and two more cuts could be coming by year-end. The ECB has also started an easing cycle. This means that project financing will be cheaper in the near future, creating a tailwind for utilities and their suppliers. Large-scale infrastructure projects, in fact, are quite sensitive to interest rates because they require debt to be carried out and executed. Utilities, too, are sensitive to interest rates, and we learned this quite well in 2022 when the market sold them off quickly as investors realized higher rates were coming.

Commodity Prices

Prysmian is exposed to commodities such as aluminum, lead, and copper. Copper alone can account for almost 30% of Prysmian's COGS. Don't think copper will be dismissed. Low- and medium-voltage distribution is still overwhelmingly copper. Data centers also utilize copper for busbars and interconnects. In fact, copper guarantees superior heat dissipation compared to other solutions. Copper is also often used in renewables integration. There are areas where aluminum is gaining ground, in particular in HV overhead lines. In addition, it is almost 3x cheaper than copper, which makes it more attractive. So, the trend is not that grids will ditch copper, but rather that they will optimize the copper and aluminum mix depending on the application and the solution that is needed. We need to be aware of this because it impacts Prysmian's revenues. This is why we see that Prysmian reports revenues both at current metal prices, which reflect the market price of the main commodities it uses, and at standard metal prices, which net out the inflationary or deflationary impact of commodities on its revenues. This also implies that Prysmian needs to hedge its exposure to key raw materials.

Geopolitics

The positive news is that Prysmian is protected from import tariffs on these commodities because it manufactures directly in the United States, sourcing domestically both copper and aluminum. In addition, tariffs on Chinese cables protect Prysmian from competition. As a matter of fact, this reason was what made me buy Prysmian convincingly back in April when tariffs were announced. The stock quickly dropped to EUR 40 a share, but to me, the panic was irrational, as Prysmian was clearly set to benefit from the new environment that was being outlined. Currently, the stock trades above EUR 80.

Prysmian: The Company

As said, Prysmian rides two secular megatrends, such as the energy transition (offshore wind, high-voltage direct current cables, renewable interconnections) and the compute transition fueled by AI (data centers build out, AI clusters). But Prysmian has not simply benefited from these trends; it has been able to effectively profit from them, outgrowing its competition and becoming the undisputed leader in its market. As it showed in its last Capital Markets Day, Prysmian's EBITDA is now 3X that of its nearest competitor, Nexans, when in 2007, when Prysmian IPOed, it was more or less aligned with it.

In addition, Prysmian distinguishes itself from the competitors both because of its global reach and the advantage of its installation fleet, which no other competitor can match. In fact, it has the largest and most advanced fleet for extended subsea cable installation campaigns. In a world that moves fast, customers can't wait for others to increase their vessels, and this allows Prysmian to further secure new orders. This is a strategic barrier to entry because it makes Prysmian a partner that customers can rely on to receive turnkey integration from design to installation.

The company has four segments: transmission, power grid, electrification, and digital solutions.

Transmission includes submarine interconnectors, offshore wind, and land. Its EBITDA CAGR projection is between 25% and 28%. What appears jaw-dropping is its backlog, which currently stands at EUR 19 billion. Considering that Prysmian converts around EUR 4 billion into annual revenue, this means that we have a backlog that extends well through 2028. The segment has an EBITDA margin of 16.7% which means that it contributes EUR 670 million to the yearly EBITDA.

Power grid spans high-voltage alternating current cables, low- and medium-voltage power distribution cables, overhead lines, and grid monitoring services.

Electrification services the industrial and construction markets, data centers, renewables, and OEMs. Electricity demand is expected to double through 2050. Today, U.S. data centers make up 6% of total power demand, but in 5 years their share should increase to 14%. Moreover, the acquisition of Encore Wire strengthens Prysmian's operation, insulating and shielding it from import tariffs. In fact, Prysmian now has enough manufacturing capacity within the United States to meet the domestic demand and source domestically most of the raw materials it needs.

Digital solutions are also fueled by the massive investments in data centers, which are currently booming. It offers, in fact, premium fiber and optical cable solutions, which are the true rails through which data traffic flows. Last year, Prysmian acquired Channel, its first major acquisition in Digital Solutions. It was a smart move because Channel is a vertically integrated U.S. manufacturer of passive equipment for telecommunication. This further strengthens Prysmian's business in the U.S.

When we look at the four segments, we see that in the next three years, transmission is expected to grow in the high double-digits, followed by digital solutions.

The company has also issued its 2028 targets and now plans on reaching around EUR 3 billion in adj. EBITDA, with yearly FCF projected to be around EUR 1.6 billion. What is quite interesting is that Prysmian is confident to compound its EPS by at least 15% a year, which is a metric that qualifies the stock for my portfolio.

This goes along with a 2028 FCF target between EUR 1.5 billion and EUR 1.7 billion, with the company's EPS expected to compound at a rate between 15% and 19% through 2028. Solutions should account for 55% of total revenues, and the ROCE should be above 20%.

We talked about two acquisitions that Prysmian made. To finance them, the company took on more debt. But its financial structure is still solid, and thanks to its strong FCF generation, the company will cut its debt in half in just two years. Looking at the numbers and the trends we have considered so far, the company has all it needs to achieve this target.

Let's look at the latest financials that Prysmian released. At the end of the first six months of this year, Prysmian reported revenues up 4% to EUR 9.65 billion and the adj. EBITDA up 30.3% YoY to EUR 1.13 billion. The adj. EBITDA margin at standard metal prices was 13.8%, which positions Prysmian more or less in line with its peers. To this extent, the industry sees similar profitability metrics among its main players.

When we look at the half-year results by segment, we have the chance to better understand where Prysmian is seeing higher profitability. Transmission is the profitability leader, and its projected growth will greatly benefit the overall mix. Electrification is the least profitable segment, with margins around 11%. Since it is also the largest segment by revenue, it is margin-dilutive right now. But since Prysmian's revenues are expected to shift toward a higher contribution of transmission and digital solutions, we can expect Prysmian's EBITDA margin to increase gradually year after year.

Thanks to its strong 1H25 results, Prysmian also upgraded its FY25 outlook, now expecting this year's EBITDA to be around EUR 2.34 billion and its FCF to top EUR 1 billion.

Valuation

There is no doubt that Prysmian is in the right industry. But the question that now arises is whether Prysmian is still a buy, especially after having doubled in just five months.

Well, the company trades at a fwd P/E of around 21. Its fwd EV/EBITDA ratio is 11.7. Its PEG ratio is around 1.7. So, the stock has substantially appreciated since its April lows and now seems to have reached fair multiples. I don't think they are stretched, given the company we are dealing with, but they are not extremely cheap.

Since Prysmian has given us some visibility on its next few years and its backlog seems to ensure us that these targets will be met, if not exceeded, let's use its FCF guidance to run a DCF model. We need to expect around EUR 1.05 billion in FCF this year, which will then grow to around EUR 1.5 billion (low-end of the guidance) by 2028. This will be done with a 12.50% growth rate. I expect it to continue for two more years, as I see the megatrends we dealt with as deeply entrenched in our economy and in their early innings. After these five years of sustained growth, the company's perpetual FCF growth rate will be 2%. Well, the result is that a share of Prysmian is probably worth around 110 EUR. Currently, we are around EUR 82 in Milan (the ADR has a 0.5 ratio, which explains why the price is around half of what we see in Milan).

This explains why I am not selling Prysmian right now, as I expect we are in the early stages of secular megatrends that will unfold for several years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Meta's Pipeline Sets The Stage For Its Next Bull Run
data.description:
Since my first analysis, Meta Platforms, Inc. (NASDAQ:META) is up 32.92%, significantly outpacing the broader sector. Perhaps I was a little ahead of myself in that analysis as the stock continued to dip before the market caught its breath.

Today, the stock trades around $780 per share, delivering 32.49% growth YTD. I wouldn’t blame you for wondering if you’ve missed the boat, as the stock has moved sideways recently, perhaps driven by a certain regulatory concern.

That said, taking a closer look at what’s actually happening within the business, the company appears to be just getting started with some impressive growth opportunities in the pipeline. For all of the reasons mentioned in this analysis, my Strong Buy rating for Meta Platforms remains unchanged.

Potential Risk: FTC Antitrust Threatens Potential Breakup of Instagram and WhatsApp

We can’t have a frank discussion about Meta without addressing the 500-pound elephant in the room, so let’s start with its FTC antitrust trial. The Federal Trade Commission is currently pursuing an antitrust trial that could, in theory, force CEO Mark Zuckerberg to spin off Instagram and WhatsApp into separate companies. I believe this is the biggest immediate threat to Meta and is the primary driver of the current “wait and see” sentiment.

For those who haven’t followed the saga, Facebook acquired Instagram for ~$1 billion back in 2012. It seemed expensive at that time, but now, it looks like the deal of the century. Then, in 2014, Facebook paid another $19 billion for WhatsApp, and that too, made everyone’s jaw drop.

The interesting thing is that the FTC reviewed both deals and gave Facebook the green light, saying that these acquisitions are fine. Today, however, the same FTC is trying to unwind the acquisition that was completed over a decade ago.

Did they suddenly change their mind after Meta spent billions integrating these platforms? This puts Meta in a difficult position because both platforms are no longer just separate apps that Meta happens to own. In fact, these are now advertising platforms that are completely interwoven - users can run ad campaigns across all three platforms from a single dashboard.

The trial started in April 2025 in Washington and concluded in May, with Zuckerberg himself even testifying.

If FTC ultimately wins the case, separating WhatsApp and Instagram would be the most likely outcome, but doing so wouldn’t be as simple as a brand spinning off from a conglomerate; it would disrupt the foundation of Meta’s advertising and data systems that drive profitability. These are decades-old integrations, so unwinding these would likely result in operational struggles that could drastically affect shareholder value.

However, I see a silver lining, and this is probably one of the reasons many investors aren’t sleeping on Meta - yes, the stock has gone sideways, but it’s far from spiraling out of control. The judge who oversees the case, Judge James Boasberg, has already shown skepticism about the FTC’s arguments during preliminary rulings.

As The Hill’s report suggests:

The judge has expressed some reservations about the way the FTC is trying to demonstrate its market definition, but obviously the underlying issue is monopoly power... It’s a really tough case in this regard because … there seems to be no qualitative evidence that’s really going to answer the question,

It’s worth noting that undoing decade-old mergers that were previously approved is virtually nonexistent in legal precedents. Also, the FTC has to prove that breaking up Meta would actually benefit consumers, and that’s a tough sell when all these apps are free to use.

Plus, TikTok, YouTube, Snapchat, and other platforms are considered Meta’s competitors, so the monopoly argument is likely moot. I personally believe the probability of a forced separation is quite low, perhaps less than 10%. I mean, the FTC is fighting yesterday’s battle.

With that risk out of the way, several factors suggest a bright future for Meta Platforms - for which we can refer to their most recent report.

Strong Q2 ‘25 Financials

Meta’s Q2 ‘25 earnings were quite impressive. Compared to the same period last year, revenue rose 22% to $47.52 billion, beating market expectations by 5.98%. But what’s more notable isn’t the actual revenue growth itself, but how revenue is growing. The acceleration stems from AI-driven improvements in ad targeting and better conversion rates, which made Meta’s advertising services more valuable to businesses compared to a year ago. In English? Meta is not only an ad-company, but is transforming itself into an AI company that sells ads.

Not only that, but gross profit also rose 23% to $39 billion, indicating that Meta is selling more ads while maintaining its pricing power. In the tech business, growing volume and maintaining prices are two Oscar-like categories. The result is that the competition isn’t negatively affecting your pricing power, and customers see enough value to pay premium prices.

On the other hand, operating income has improved 38% to $20.4 billion, with an operating margin expanding to 43%, up from 38% a year ago. This is all thanks to the significantly better operating leverage. Meta has already built the AI systems and the infrastructure, so additional revenue drops almost straight to the bottom line.

Meta’s margin is something that most mature companies can’t reach. Apple (AAPL), for example, has maintained margins around 30% over the same period, despite selling premium-priced products, while Alphabet’s (GOOGL) margin is 32.43% - one of Meta’s closest competitors. Meta’s margin expansion indicates business is getting more efficient as it scales - another Oscar category.

Net income also jumped 36% to $18.34 billion, which translated to a basic EPS of $7.28 per share, up from $5.31 in the same period last year. Meta has been smart about share repurchases when the stock was under pressure - and no doubt helped prop up the stock. Meanwhile, diluted non-GAAP EPS came in at $7.14, which notably outpaced market estimates by 21.82%.

Business Metrics

In terms of user metrics, Daily Active People, or DAP, rose 6.4% to 3.48 billion, and it’s gradually increasing. Adding 6% to the current base means they brought in roughly 200 million new daily users this quarter, which is wildly impressive.

But the one thing I believe has been overlooked is Meta AI, specifically, the company’s digital assistant. Meta AI now has ~1 billion monthly active users, which is up over 40% from the reported 700 million users in January 2025. In just a few months, they managed to add over 300 million users to a new AI product by integrating their AI assistant across WhatsApp, Facebook, and Instagram. With this approach, users don’t have to download a new app or learn a new interface.

AI-Powered Smart Glasses

Now, we come to what might be the most exciting development, although it’s received the least attention from Wall Street.

Meta revealed its new Meta Ray-Ban Display glasses at the September 17 Connect event, with a starting price of $799, and is set to launch on September 30 this year. It’s something that’s been promised for decades - smart glasses that people will actually want to wear.

Alphabet tried this with Google Glass only to fail spectacularly, and even Apple’s Apple Vision Pro didn’t live up to the hype. Meta, however, partnered with Ray-Ban, so these would only look like stylish sunglasses, not something that would make you look like a cyborg.

That said, it’s not just about the looks. These glasses have an AI assistant, speakers, microphones, cameras, and augmented reality features to extend Meta’s platforms into the world.

Let’s say you’re walking through Rome, the AI assistant can tell you information about the historic buildings you’re looking at, or give you a real-time translation of signage, or even record your kid’s football game without fumbling for your phone.

Each pair comes with a Neural Band controller, which makes it even more futuristic. It uses electromyography technology, or EMG, to pick up electrical signals between your brain and hand movements. Basically, you can control the digital interfaces with hand gestures. I know it sounds like straight from a Sci-Fi movie, but today, it’s a reality.

There are also signs of market validation. According to EssilorLuxottica, the manufacturer of Ray-Ban Meta smart glasses, its sales more than tripled in the H1 '25 compared to the same period last year. So, there’s a real consumer demand, not just some tech enthusiasts buying fancy gadgets.

WhatsApp Monetization Begins

Perhaps Meta’s biggest untapped opportunity is monetizing WhatsApp. As per recent stats, WhatsApp has over 3 billion monthly active users - over 100 million in the U.S. alone, where it’s growing rapidly. Recently, Meta also announced that businesses can now run status ads through the WhatsApp messaging features. It might not be exciting for WhatsApp users like me, but it’s a huge win for the company. I can’t say I’m surprised, though. Actually, I’m only surprised it took them this long to start.

You see, WhatsApp has always been the odd one out in Meta’s family. Facebook and Instagram were driving profits, while this green app remains largely ad-free. Perhaps, with respect to its promise to users about maintaining a clean messaging experience.

But Meta is placing them in the Status feature, since most users already expect to see updates from friends and businesses anyway. So they won’t be shoving ads into our conversations. The Updates tab, where Status can be seen, is reportedly used by 1.5 billion people every day, which makes it one of the most valuable digital real estate right now.

Industry experts even suggested WhatsApp’s ad revenue could add over $10 billion in annual revenue by as early as 2028. But I think that’s still on the conservative side. Let’s do some quick math; if the app’s $3 billion users could generate just $5 per year in ad revenue, that’s $15 billion. Meanwhile, Facebook generates over $60 per user annually in the U.S. and Canada based on available data.

Meta hasn’t released any recent data about the metric yet, but even if WhatsApp reaches only 10% of Facebook’s monetization level, that would already be a notable revenue driver for the company.

META Stock Profile

At the time of this publication, META stock trades at ~$786 per share. However, many are concerned with the company’s valuation. Right now, its forward price-earnings (GAAP) is at 27.73x, which is about 41% more expensive than the sector median of 19.68x - which I think is justified. In fact, I’m expecting a much higher multiple from one of the most valuable and proven companies over the last decade.

The stock is up 34% YTD and 211% over the past five years, but I believe we’re still in the middle innings of Meta’s AI era. Looking back, the company has successfully shifted from desktop to mobile. At this point, I believe it's expanding from mobile to AI and augmented reality, and each platform transformation has expanded Meta’s total addressable market.

At today's levels, it seems like the market is still valuing Meta primarily as a social media advertising company, and most of its potential growth drivers aren’t yet priced in. WhatsApp, for example, could bring tremendous growth to the business, but very few are talking about it. And there’s a lot more potential growth drivers to mention.

Investment Verdict

I believe Meta Platforms is at an inflection point. Its AI is now driving real financial growth, having moved beyond an experimental phase. The improvements in ad targeting are also reflected in the revenue growth and margin expansion.

Sure, there’s the FTC trial risk, and yes, the competition from TikTok and other platforms will only intensify, but Meta has also proven time and again that it can adapt to challenges. So, even as a shareholder, these risks don’t keep me awake at night.

For Meta’s market position, financial performance, and track record, and all the growth catalysts in the pipelines, I’m reaffirming my META Strong Buy rating.

data.title: Nanobiotix: Today's Data Readout For Radiation Therapy Enhances Bull Case
data.description:
Investment Overview

Shares of Nanobiotix S.A. (NASDAQ:NBTX) are buoyant today—up ~25% in trading so far—after the Paris, France-based biotech reported results for its drug candidate NBTXR3/SBRT in combination with immune checkpoint inhibitors ("ICIs") in anti-PD-1 resistant patients with melanoma treated in the phase I trial Study 1100.

Before we take a closer look at the data and implications for the company and its partner Janssen, the drug development subsidiary of Pharma giant Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), I'll provide a brief overview of the company:

Nanobiotix—Company Overview

According to Nanobiotix's 2024 annual report/20-F filing:

We were founded as a spin-off from the State University of New York, Buffalo, in 2003. Team members at Nanobiotix, including our founder, Laurent Levy, have two decades of experience developing Nanobiotix’s technology and we believe we are a pioneer and leader in the field of nanomedicine. We have built an integrated, multidisciplinary team that combines expertise in physics, biology and medicine. Our corporate headquarters and manufacturing facilities are located in Paris, France, with U.S. operations in Cambridge, Massachusetts.

Nanobiotix listed on the Nasdaq in December 2020, according to a press release:

by way of a capital increase of 7,300,000 new ordinary shares, consisting of a public offering of 5,445,000 ordinary shares in the form of American Depositary Shares, each representing the right to receive one ordinary share, in the United States and a concurrent offering of 1,855,000 ordinary shares in certain jurisdictions outside of the United States to certain investors. The offering price was set at $13.50 per ADS in the U.S. The aggregate gross proceeds are expected to be approximately $98.6 million.

According to its 20-F:

We have three platforms that each seek to bring the benefits of nanotechnology to human medical problems:

  • NBTXR3 (JNJ-19003), our potentially first-in-class radioenhancer designed to enhance radiation therapy.
  • Curadigm, our nanoprimer technology is designed to enhance the therapeutic index.
  • Oocuity, our platform designed to help central nervous system (CNS) based ailments.

Our most advanced platform has produced our lead product candidate, NBTXR3, which is designed to improve local control of solid tumors by increasing the effect of radiotherapy in the tumor without increasing damage to surrounding healthy tissues, and to improve systemic control through its potential immune priming effect subsequent to the physical destruction of the tumor caused by the physics-based mechanism of action ("MoA").

Nanobiotix's partnership with Janssen is worth up to $2.6bn in milestone payments. The therapy is undergoing a Phase 3 study in head and neck cancer, with interim data "that could potentially lead to registration," i.e., a filing for commercial approval, and also a Phase 2 study in stage III lung cancer and multiple Phase 1/2 studies in various solid tumors.

NBTXR3 is a radiotherapy, which Nanobiotix states (in a corporate presentation) is "one of the largest markets in oncology," with up to 12m patients treated with a radiotherapy per annum, compared to just 0.4m treated with a PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor—Merck & Co., Inc.'s (MRK) PD-1 drug Keytruda was the world's best-selling prescription drug last year, earning $29.5bn of revenues.

The MoA of NBTXR3 is discussed in the two slides below:

Nanobiotix says that proof-of-concept was established when NBTXR3 was able to double pathological complete response rates in a randomised Phase 2/3 study. According to the company, the therapy:

  • Achieved its primary endpoint of pathological CRR.
  • Achieved its secondary endpoint in quality of margins (R0).
  • Demonstrated long-term persistent bioavailability.
  • No impact on the patient's ability to receive the planned dose of RT.

Significance Of Today's Data

We might wonder why, when lung and head and neck ("HNSCC") cancer studies are further advanced, the market is celebrating Phase 1 data?

The data comes from Study 1100, expansion cohort 3 - cohorts 1 and 2 were both evaluating HNSCC, but the third looked at patients with melanoma resistant to treatment with an anti-PD-1 or anti-PDL1 drug, i.e., Keytruda or Bristol-Myers Squibb Company's (BMY) Opdivo. Nanobiotix says that:

In this resistant patient population and with no standard approach, response rate is generally low (e.g., 20% ORR and 50% DCR, respectively.

There were 21 patients in this study, and only one patient experienced a grade 3 or worse treatment-emergent adverse safety event ("TEAE"), and only 24% of patients experienced a TEAE of any kind. Many patients had failed on multiple different types of treatment, including anti-CTLA4, BRAF inhibitors, and anti-LAG3 - all well-established targets for melanoma.

As we can see above (slide taken from today's study results presentation), the objective response rate ("ORR") was 47.4%, and the disease control rate was 78.9%, with four complete responses ("CR") also observed. The slide below provides an impressive overview of each patient's prior therapies and response after treatment with NBTXR3:

The median overall survival ("OS") rate was 14.6 months at data cut-off. I believe the market has greeted this data with enthusiasm for several reasons. Firstly, there are very few, if any options for the types of patients treated in this study. On a conference call today, Nanobiotix's CEO Laurent Levy told analysts:

When you look at those patients, they have received several lines of treatment. So they pretty much have exhausted for most of them any other possibility. So the real -- in real life for those patients, that will be almost nothing or any palliative care that the doc could give the patient.

In that sense, then, NBTXR3 could be a viable treatment of last resort, but equally, obtaining such strong results in such a late-stage patient population opens up the tantalizing prospect of the therapy successfully treating earlier-stage patients—as the CEO put it:

But in melanoma precisely, it is sure that if we could have an action at the beginning of the disease and not wait for the patient to fail multiple lines, we should potentially get a much better outcome than what we see here.

Furthermore, data providing evidence that a therapy works is always welcome and will doubtless do no harm to how the market rates the success chances of Nanobiotix's other programs—ditto the impressive safety profile.

Looking Ahead: Upcoming Catalysts, Approval, Commercial Prospects

In the slide below, Nanobiotix lists upcoming catalysts that could trigger milestone payouts from Janssen as follows:

When the company reported its fiscal end-of-year results for 2024 in April this year, the company reported a net loss for the year of €(68m) and cash of €50m, so JNJ's milestones feel essential in terms of Nanobiotix being able to complete its late-stage studies. In fact, in March this year, the company altered the terms of its deal with Janssen so that Janssen funds its Phase 3 study in HNSCC, although the company also noted:

We are continuing to evaluate additional, preferably non-dilutive, financial solutions to further extend runway into 2027 and ensure our path to sustainable revenue through the hundreds of millions in potential milestone payments related to our lead programs expected in the coming year.

I would not be at all surprised if, on the back of today's data, the company moved to raise funds via a share offering, which will dilute existing investors and therefore potentially erase some share price gains made today.

In terms of the market opportunity, I doubt management has too many worries on that score, given it estimates that HNSCC and lung cancer approvals would open up a $10bn market opportunity and, of course, result in billions of revenues from Janssen.

In terms of competition, naturally, in oncology indications, it is likely to be intense. In melanoma, drugs such as Iovance Biotherapeutics, Inc.'s (IOVA) Amtagvi are approved to treat late-stage melanoma, and Merck & Moderna are developing a melanoma "vaccine" to work in combo with Keytruda. In lung and HNSCC, while ICIs are generally the standard of care, the sector is teeming with drug candidates with new mechanisms of action - from antibody drug conjugates, bispecifics, cell therapies, and rival radiopharmaceutical candidates.

Concluding Thoughts: A Strong Readout And A Promising New Approach Make A Decent Bull Case

The data shared by Nanobiotix today appears to be undeniably impressive, and given the company's market cap is just $513m at the time of writing, further upside ought to be achievable. However, for anyone thinking of investing today, it is worth pointing out that biotech valuations typically drift downwards when there are no near-term catalysts in play, so it may be worth keeping your powder dry and capitalizing on this in a few weeks or months' time.

With that said, Nanobiotix does have more catalysts in play, for example, Phase 3 head and neck due in 1H26, an esophageal cancer data update, and a first patient injected in a non-small lung cancer ("NSCLC") Phase 3 study (it's unclear when we will see interim data.

There are absolutely risks to consider with Nanobiotix—the company would be somewhat lost without Janssen and is heavily reliant on its ongoing partnership (although if it can raise funds on the back of today's data that would be a significant boost), more mature data is required to test durability of response, and the company may ultimately need to drop some programs and prioritize others in order to manage its finances and make sure it does not exhaust its funding runway.

Nevertheless, all things considered, this feels like a pretty good day for Nanobiotix and its shareholders, and with radiopharmaceuticals being a "hot" space for Big Pharma, I would not be surprised to see one make a bid for this particular biotech, and that bid would almost certainly be at a >$1bn valuation, in my view.

All things considered, then, this slightly under-the-radar Parisian biotech deserves to be allocated a Buy rating, in my view.

data.title: Contrarian Take: We Didn't Hate The Apple Event (Long-Term Buy)
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Tech Contrarians as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

This is the year to buy Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL) for the longer term, not the year to see meaningful returns manifest, in our opinion. The Apple Event held last week confirmed that sentiment. We received a series of announcements for new iPhones, Apple Watches, and AirPods, all set to hit shelves on September 19th, i.e., this Friday, in what is still a lukewarm demand environment (at best).

The market definitely didn't seem too impressed with Apple trading 3% lower the day after, as shown below. The stock has recovered since, up from $226 to $238 at Tuesday's close, and we see more room for upside into year's end.

We’re seeing some positive signs from Apple: management flirting with a potential deal with Google's (GOOGL) (GOOG) Gemini to power a revamp of Siri voice assistance, plus the fact that the company will continue to enjoy $20B annually from Google for Chrome to be the default engine. What we need now are more signs that the tech is competitive (or will be soon) enough to incentivize refresh cycles in North America in a muddied economic backdrop and, more importantly, enough to attract Chinese customers from other cheaper, even AI-powered, alternatives.

Easter eggs at this year's event

This year’s event gave us a little more of that than the iPhone 16 debut did, but still didn’t seem to warrant an “ah-ha” moment, at least insofar as stock price reaction. We don’t think the event was as big a flop as the market priced in last Wednesday. We see an attractive window to add, as we think the market overreacted if you look at the innovation-to-price ratio.

Here are the updates we got:

  • AirPods Pro 3 priced at $249 - new built-in heart rate sensor, live translation, twice as much noise cancellation, better sound, and a swappable ear tip option.

  • iPhone Air priced at $999 - a slimmed-down iPhone with a 6.5-inch ProMotion display and about 5.3mm in thickness. It’s Apple’s most durable design yet, with a ceramic shield that hugs a titanium frame. It has a single 48MP fusion camera and A19 Pro processor with an in-house C1X modem and what Apple has called an “all-day battery life.”

  • iPhone 17, iPhone 17 Pro, and iPhone 17 Pro Max priced at $799, $1,099, $1,199 - the standard iPhone now has a wider display, 6.3 inches, and an adaptive refresh rate of up to 120Hz. The iPhone 17 Pro also has that titanium frame with ceramic shield.

  • Apple Watch SE 3, Series 11, and Ultra 3 are priced at $249, $399, and $799 - all three will launch with WatchOS 26, meaning they’ll have a liquid glass design, plus an AI-powered Workout Buddy.

The market is right in that this event and the last couple of events, for that matter, haven’t been delivering Steve Jobs-like jaw-dropping innovation, but guess what? That’s why we’re not seeing the products get priced like jaw-dropping innovative products. If you take a look at the price of the iPhone upon launch over the chart below (which is not all-inclusive, as it cuts off in 2022), it tells a story about the innovation-to-price ratio of Apple.

Last year, Apple’s iPhone 16 lineup was priced as follows: “the base iPhone 16 for $829, the iPhone 16 Plus at $899, the iPhone 16 Pro at $999, and the iPhone 16 Pro Max at $1,199.” That comes in contrast to this year’s pricing with the iPhone 17, with the base version at $799, the Pro Max at $1,099, and the iPhone Pro Max at $1,199. The higher-end models are more expensive, but the base model price hasn’t moved. In fact, it is lower than it was a year ago.

The chart below indicates a downward trend in terms of pricing power. So, while we didn’t get the ah-ha moment, I would say we’re still getting charged a solid performance-price ratio from Apple, which comes with both an ecosystem and the high-end status.

Not to mention that we were all watching for a price hike at this event. A lot of the chatter heading into the event was, “Will the iPhone go up in price?” in the words of Jeff Fieldhack from CounterPoint Research. Morgan Stanley analyst Erik Woodring expected the company to “modestly raise iPhone prices for the first time in seven years” heading into the event. Part of why the market was expecting price hikes was due to the tariff-tense environment and the $600B commitment Apple has to pay to get exempt. This all shows that although there was room for a sharper price hike, we didn’t see one.

We did, however, see signs of moving towards an ah-ha moment of innovation. The iPhone Air basically sets the base case for the foldable phone. It’s no secret that competition like Samsung, Google, Motorola, and Vivo have beaten Apple to the bush here, but their models have overall given foldable smartphone tech a bad reputation with shorter life spans, creases, and higher screen fragility. The iPhone Air presents slimness not at the expense of durability or battery capacity, which is 27 hours better than expected, although 3 hours lower than the iPhone 17. This is a step in the right direction.

The other factor to consider is the ecosystem that Apple continues to build out, which feeds into its booming service segment. The company is disciplinedly monetizing its installed base of 2.4B iOS users. Dan Ives estimates there are “roughly 315 million iPhone users of 1.5 billion users worldwide who have not upgraded their phones in over 4 years, speaking to an upgrade opportunity on the horizon for Cupertino with China front and center.” We should be seeing new features launch, particularly around Apple Intelligence, which will further expand that ecosystem.

A rate cut could only help

Things are also looking up in terms of the macro backdrop, with the market anticipating an imminent rate cut, one that we received earlier today (September 17th) with Fed Chairman Jerome Powell announcing a 25 basis point cut. His commentary noted that they're "starting with a 25 basis point cut," which means there are more to come but not as many as the market hoped for. The concern is that the market has priced in too many rate cuts this year, and that needs to be corrected. The overall positive, however, of rates coming down is still there, and this should translate to healthier upgrade cycles for consumers. PPI data that came out last week increased 2.6% in August, after growing 3.1% in July, which triggered Trump to post on the need for a big and immediate rate cut. We’re seeing more overall chatter about a rate cut after preliminary benchmark reviews pointed to the fact that “the US economy added 911,000 fewer jobs in the 12 months through March 2025 than initially reported.”

We expect the macro backdrop to begin to recover in the next year and turn more favorable for smartphone end demand. Earlier this year, the IDC lowered its forecast from 2.3% smartphone growth this year to 0.6%. Now, as of late August, we’ve seen this number slightly revised up to 1%, supported by 3.9% growth in iOS during the year. Hence, while we recognize the tense macro backdrop, we do believe that we'll see an overall healthier consumer spend in 2026.

Potential risk exists

Even in a healthier macro environment, the lingering headwind to Apple that we have yet to see an implemented solution to is China. The company's sales have struggled in the Chinese market, dropping as much as 11% earlier this year in 1Q25. The risk in the Chinese market comes from heavy Chinese competition with pricing power and AI features that Apple lacks. This is something we're keeping an eye on. We're beginning to see the trend reverse, with sales growing 4.4% in 3Q25.

This morning, Apple's sales numbers from China heading into the iPhone 17 launch came out and showed a 6% drop from a year ago, which signals weaker-than-usual sales heading into a new product release. The concern to us was that Apple is losing ground, but the good news is that competitors, including Xiaomi, Vivo, and Honor Devices, all experienced a drop in the first eight weeks of the quarter, too. The chart below showcases smartphone sales. So, all in all, we think this is a problem, but one that's being resolved.

Bottom Line

We have faith in Apple’s long-term story and think weakness is the time to buy. The stock is up ~3% over the past month and 2% YTD. It’s safe to say Apple hasn’t been an easy stock to own this year. Having said that, the stock usually trades down post-launch before recovering into 4Q, and we think we could see this play out again.

In terms of valuation, it’s also not cheap. The stock trades at a forward P/E of 32.25x against the peer group average of 24.68x and forward EV/Sales of 8.44x versus the group average of 3.44x. We do think valuation isn’t a reason to shy away, considering the next thing we’re all expecting out of Apple is a deal on where their AI integration is going, which should move forward both the stock price and innovation rate. The worst has been priced in, but the positive potential has yet to be considered, mainly an anticipated foldable phone and AI updates, which would all come with a higher average selling price that the market could stomach, considering we’re likely to see more favorable interest rates. Hence, we’re marking this as a window to buy.

data.title: Virtus Silvant Focused Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The second quarter began with a whimper and ended with a bang for U.S. large-cap growth stocks. Markets initially extended first-quarter weakness, falling to new lows in early April amid rising inflation concerns and broader economic worries following the announcement of sweeping new U.S. tariffs on major trading partners. However, the sharp selloff reversed quickly, driven by a 90-day pause in tariff implementation and growing belief that the proposed levies ultimately would be further delayed or notably scaled back, used more as negotiation leverage than enacted in full. By quarter end, markets had not only recovered earlier lost ground but rallied to all-time highs, also overcoming last quarter’s steep decline and moving higher year to date, capping what was, in effect, a policy-driven roller coaster.

The Russell 1000® Growth Index rallied 17.84% for the quarter, led by the utilities (+64.26%), information technology (+25.05%), and communication services (+23.88%) sectors. Energy (-9.66%) and healthcare (-1.98%) were the only sectors to decline.

Performance

The Virtus Silvant Focused Growth Fund returned +20.87% (Class INST) (MUTF:PGFIX) for the quarter, compared with the Russell 1000 Growth Index’s rise of 17.84%. Stock selection in consumer discretionary and industrials and a zero-weight in consumer staples benefited performance. Stock selection in healthcare stock weighed on returns.

NVIDIA (NVDA) and Microsoft (MSFT) were among the top stock contributors.

  • Artificial intelligence (AI) chipmaker NVIDIA sharply rebounded, fueled by a beat-and-raise quarter with 69% revenue growth and continued leadership in AI processing. While near-term uncertainty around China-related sales persisted, demand for its new Blackwell GPUs remained exceptionally strong. We added to the position based on the company’s upbeat outlook and positive key metrics.
  • Technology conglomerate Microsoft outperformed, driven by revenue strength led by accelerating Azure growth from increasing AI workloads, signaling that the firm’s AI strategy, powered by its OpenAI partnership and Copilot integration, is exceeding expectations. We added to the position, given strong performance across all tracked segments and the success of its early adoption of an AI platform across multiple products, which is paying off in both pricing and rapidly growing usage.

UnitedHealth Group (UNH) and Eli Lilly (LLY) were among the largest stock detractors.

  • Health insurer UnitedHealth Group fell following a weak earnings and revenue report, despite a better-than-expected key metric membership loss ratio. Elevated care utilization, particularly in its Medicare Advantage business, and challenges in the Optum segment, pressured results; the CEO subsequently resigned, and the company withdrew its 2025 guidance. Due to these execution issues, we sold the position, having previously reduced it in December.
  • Pharmaceutical firm Eli Lilly lagged despite strong earnings and positive Phase III data for its oral GLP-1 Orforglipron, targeting type 2 diabetes and weight loss. The weakness stemmed from CVS’s selection of competitor Wegovy as its preferred obesity drug, raising concerns about pricing pressure and other exclusive pharmacy benefit manager deals. We held steady with the stock, as the company’s 2025 guidance already factored in pricing declines, and its superior Zepbound plus Orforglipron’s launch should help secure its leadership in the $100 billion-plus obesity market.

Outlook

At this stage of the cycle, one of the primary questions is whether inflation from tariffs will slow growth materially this year. While these effects take time to work through, we currently do not expect a slowdown severe enough to trigger a recession. Some drag on growth is likely, but the extent remains uncertain. Earnings continue to beat expectations, especially in large-cap tech and communication services, where estimate revisions have driven strong gains. Second quarter GDP looks to be around 2.5%, rebounding from first quarter softness, and while second-half estimates are being trimmed, they remain positive. Additionally, the recent “Big Beautiful Bill” tax and spending bill adds tax cut and debt ceiling clarity, removing some policy overhang, though it may create fiscal headwinds due to its deficit impact. While we remain mindful of these macro risks, particularly inflation and Federal Reserve policy, we maintain our bottom-up focus and continue to find opportunities in fundamentally strong companies, especially in AI-related areas of tech and communication services, where growth continues to be attractive.
